transcript.content:
You are a political lean classifier. Classify the article's political lean using the 5-class AllSides scale. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "lean": "Left"|"Lean Left"|"Center"|"Lean Right"|"Right",
  "confidence": 0.0 to 1.0,
  "reasoning": "string",
  "key_indicators": ["string"]
}
Scale:
  Left: strong consistent left-leaning editorial choices
  Lean Left: moderate left tilt, some balance present
  Center: balanced, neither side consistently favored
  Lean Right: moderate right tilt, some balance present
  Right: strong consistent right-leaning editorial choices
Confidence: 0.5-0.7 subtle lean, 0.8-0.95 clear lean, 0.95+ unambiguous.

Classify the political lean of this article:

---
HEADLINE: By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S
SOURCE: Newsweek

ARTICLE:

By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S. President Donald Trump has said the U.S. is "seeking immediate negotiations" to acquire Greenland, deepening the yawning chasm opening up between Washington and its closest allies over the Arctic territory.Trump called Greenland "part of North America" and "our territory" during a rambling speech to world leaders at the World Economic Forum in the Swiss town of Davos on Wednesday.The Republican reiterated his claim that the U.S. is the only power that can protect the mostly ice-covered expanse, and insisted this would not pose a threat to NATO.Alliance officials have repeatedly said a U.S. military attack on Greenland, a semi-autonomous part of Denmark, would spell the end of NATO. The prospect of the U.S. — the most influential and powerful member of NATO —attacking territory belonging to a fellow alliance member has always been unfathomable, until now.The White House had refused to rule out using military force to take Greenland, which would be a violation of international law."I don't want to use force. I won't use force," Trump said. "All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland."This is a breaking story. More to follow....Request Reprint & LicensingSubmit CorrectionView Editorial & AI GuidelinesAdd Newsweek as a preferred source on Google to see more of our trusted coverage when you search.Related PodcastsTop StoriesFor MembersWorldWhy the US Won't Be Coming to Its Longtime Ally's Aid in Syria10 min readFor MembersNewsUncommon Knowledge: China Complaining About Trump's Land Grab? ---
